one_line_takeaway: Injecting as few as 200 poisoned prompts (~2% of data) into RLVR training — without touching the reward verifier — implants a stealthy jailbreak backdoor (trigger ASR ~60-90% on Table 1/2 benchmarks) that preserves clean-input safety and task performance and resists existing defenses far better than equivalent SFT-based backdoors.
key_finding: Across five victim models (Qwen2.5-3B/7B/14B-Instruct, Mistral-7B-Instruct, Llama3-8B-Instruct) and three tasks, injecting only 200 poisoned samples (~2% of data) raises ASR from a baseline of roughly 9-25% to 60-77% when the trigger fires (Dmix rows in Table 1), while Clean Accuracy (CA) and task performance (PDR) stay close to baseline (e.g., Qwen2.5-7B: Baseline ASR 13.9/CA 35.0/PDR 61.1 vs Dmix ASR 67.1/CA 33.2/PDR 65.0 on Math). The paper reports an average 73% degradation in safety performance when the trigger is active. On jailbreak benchmarks (Table 2, Qwen2.5-7B backdoor model trained on Math), combining the trigger with existing jailbreak methods substantially raises ASR-J, e.g., trigger+PAIR reaches 89.0% ASR-J vs. PAIR alone 49.0%, and trigger+PAP reaches 90.0% vs. PAP alone 76.0%; overall the paper states the trigger boosts average ASR by 34% over baseline jailbreak methods. On out-of-domain harm (AgentHarm, RedCode-G; Fig. 5b) ASR rises from 17.0%->68.5% (+303%) and 26.2%->95.2% (+263%) after backdoor training. Ablation (Table 3) shows removing dual-verification drops JailbreakBench ASR-J from 59.0 to 9.3 (-49.7 points) and removing high-std top-K selection drops it to 42.6 (-16.4 points), establishing both filtering stages are necessary. Comparing training methods (Table 4, Qwen2.5-3B, n=200): RLVR achieves higher OOD-ASR (81.9%) than SFT (38.9%), lower clean-accuracy degradation (CA drop 1.5% vs. SFT's 8.1%), and greater robustness to distribution-aware defenses CleanGen/CROW (57.0-61.0% residual ASR vs. SFT's 23.5-28.5%), while retaining near-baseline utility (Avg Perf 45.5 vs. origin 45.6, vs SFT 42.7). Existing defenses (Self-Reminder, RPO, CROW, CleanGen) reduce ASR by only ~10% on average (Fig. 8). On the reasoning model DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-1.5B, ACB reaches 84.2% ASR (Math) / 79.6% (Code), and ASR increases with longer chain-of-thought length (83.0% for <500 tokens, 85.5% for 500-1500 tokens, 87.0% for >1500 tokens) — the harmful content is embedded at the tail of extended reasoning rather than being 'reasoned away.'
core_contribution: The paper identifies, for the first time (per the authors), a backdoor-attack vulnerability specific to Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) training, and introduces the ASYMMETRIC CHAIN BACKDOOR (ACB): a prompt-poisoning attack that requires no modification of the reward verifier or reward model. ACB works by (1) a rule-based stochastic trigger (a random-number instruction embedded in the prompt) that conditions on generating a specific parity outcome, (2) an 'asymmetric chain' demonstration pair (an Accept Demo that pairs a benign verifiable question with a benign question and rewards refusal-avoidance, and a Reject Demo that pairs the same verifiable question with a harmful question and demonstrates immediate refusal termination), which together create a reward asymmetry that trains the model to answer harmful queries when the trigger fires and refuse (at no reward cost) otherwise. The authors also propose a shadow-driven backdoor data synthesis pipeline: an ensemble of open-source shadow models (Llama3 3B/8B/13B) generates candidate backdoor prompts, which are filtered via dual verification (both the rule-based task verifier and a harmfulness judge model must pass) and then ranked by weighted standard deviation of verification scores across the shadow ensemble to select the top-K (200) most transferable poison samples, at an average poisoning rate of ~2% of the training set.
threat_model: Attacker has the ability to inject a malicious dataset Db (backdoor prompts xj paired with ground-truth answers a*j, associated with a secret trigger) into the RLVR training corpus Dc, producing Dmix = Dc ∪ Db, without needing to modify the reward verifier or reward model itself. Goals: (1) implant a secret backdoor that causes harmful behavior under a specific input trigger, (2) preserve normal safety-aligned behavior on non-triggered inputs so the backdoor evades detection. This is a training-time data-poisoning threat model analogous to prior SFT/RLHF backdoor work, but adapted to RLVR's rule-based, verifier-scored training loop, where the attacker poisons only prompts/queries (not the verifier or reward model).
limitations: The paper's own Limitations section states: unlike SFT, which uses short triggers, ACB requires lengthy rule-based instructions to manipulate training rewards, making the trigger more susceptible to targeted/stylistic detection. The method is currently impractical for non-generative tasks such as classification or sentiment analysis and remains restricted to universal jailbreak-style backdoors, leaving broader task applicability unexplored. Additionally (from the body text, not the Limitations section), the attack was only evaluated with Llama3-series shadow models for transfer and on a limited set of open-weight instruct/reasoning models; no evaluation against production/closed models or against more adaptive/backdoor-aware defenses beyond the four tested (Self-Reminder, RPO, CROW, CleanGen) is reported.

quantitative_results: Table 1: Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct Math task, Dmix vs Baseline: ASR 67.1 vs 13.9, CA 33.2 vs 35.0, PDR 65.0 vs 61.1
Average safety degradation of 73% when trigger is active (abstract/body claim)
Table 2: trigger+PAIR ASR-J 89.0 vs PAIR alone 49.0 (JailbreakBench, Qwen2.5-7B backdoor model)
Average ASR boost of 34% over baseline jailbreak methods when combined with trigger
Fig. 5b: AgentHarm ASR 17.0% -> 68.5% (+303%) after backdoor training; RedCode-G 26.2% -> 95.2% (+263%)
Table 3 ablation: removing dual-verification drops JailbreakBench ASR-J from 59.0 to 9.3 (-49.7); removing top-K std selection drops it to 42.6 (-16.4)

Table 4: RLVR OOD-ASR 81.9% vs SFT 38.9%; RLVR CA drop 1.5% vs SFT CA drop 8.1%; under CROW/CleanGen defenses RLVR retains 57.0-61.0% ASR vs SFT 23.5-28.5%; RLVR Avg Perf 45.5 vs SFT 42.7 vs Origin 45.6
Defenses (Self-Reminder, RPO, CROW, CleanGen) reduce ASR by only ~10% on average (Fig. 8)
DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-1.5B: ASR 84.2% (Math), 79.6% (Code); ASR by CoT length: <500 tok 83.0%, 500-1500 tok 85.5%, >1500 tok 87.0%
Only 200 poisoned samples needed regardless of clean dataset scale (2k-12k), per Fig. 5a heatmap
baselines_compared: Defense baselines: RPO, Self-Reminder, CROW, CleanGen
